speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape one. The survivor, Leo Zapiler, October 22, 1996, interviewer Toni Katsch Binstock. Interview is being conducted in Denver, Colorado, United States of America, and is being done in English. My name is Toni Katsch Binstock. Today is October 22, 1996. I'm honored to be interviewing Leo Zapiler in Denver, Colorado, United States of America. And the interview will be conducted in English. Would you give me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Leo Zapiler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ela Leo Zapiler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been known by any other nicknames or any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ela Leo, two names.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 4, 1903.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today is what?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your age today? Your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age-- I born March 4, 1903.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today you are 90-what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 93 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the city where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw. Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw, Poland, Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you born near Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And describe the place that you were born. Was it a small town or city? I thought you were born--

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw is a big town. Warsaw is the capital from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever live anywhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't out longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me a little bit about where you lived when you were-- when you were a young man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I lived, mostly Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your address when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's go back to when you were very young. Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Wolina state, Wolin, Because I had relatives there. My father went to America. Before he went to America, he was remarried. And I was living there with my stepmother. It was Luboml.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother-- tell me about your mother. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember my mother. I was about four or five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't remember her? Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yochevet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father take care of you after your mother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the stepmother took care of me. And I had a brother younger from me, three years younger. And I had an older sister. She was seven years older than I am. And I had a brother which-- my oldest brother went to the service in 1914. We never heard from him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Asher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there was Asher. And then the next child was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, Freda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my father went to the army, to the service, took some seven years in the service. And after he came from the service, then I was born. It was seven years between my sister and me. It was seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was in what army? Which army? What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russian army. In the war, 1905 with the Japanese.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- you were a little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born after that. I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your stepmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruchel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about growing up? What kind of things did you do as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was going to cheder. And my father was a painter. And he painted church in-- in the-- in the village. And that's what he made a living. We had also a factory of oil, what my father built. It was running with horses, three horses all the time. Every two hours they changed. And the horses kept dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to the factory when you were a little boy? Did you go to the factory where the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you went to cheder. Did you ever go to your father's place of business with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father, summertimes he was a painter. He went to Rega and painted a bridge. He painted two years. Two or three years he painted bridge in Rega. So summertime, he was there. In wintertime, we had a factory for oil in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said there was another child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel, my little brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian. Yiddish and Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of things did your family do together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did your family do together as a family? What did you do together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't do anything together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you describe your religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was a shul. We went to the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family go together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how often did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to a trade school, or what did you do as a young boy to learn a trade?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't learn a trade. I learned a trade for myself later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in '12, '13, in 1913, so we went to Warsaw. I wasn't born in Warsaw. We went to Warsaw. My father was in New York. And I went to Warsaw. My sister went to Warsaw, my brother. My stepmother died, so we forgot this place. And in Warsaw, I start learning electric.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Electrician.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old I was, 14, 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took care of you after your stepmother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. Myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your-- your brother and sister live together in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother was in a different place. My sister was in a different place. And I was in a different. I was working by people. And that's where I lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you learned to be an electrician?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kind of jobs did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I learned mechanic. And I learned mechanic and electrician, so I got enough work to do until I met my wife and I had married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, boys and girls run around all the time. We had boys which was with them and girls would run around. So I choose one. We went every night to dance, was dancing. We went to dance, and I choose one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year were you married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your relationship, the Jews and non-Jews? Did you know any non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just like here, we know each other well, but not specials, nothing. I didn't have nothing to do with non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever go to school? Well, you went to cheder, but you never went to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just cheder. Just cheder. I didn't go to school, just cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So married-- and do you remember what day you were married in 1927?

speaker: interviewer
text: What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: After [NON-ENGLISH]. It was the summertime anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your address where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw, [NON-ENGLISH] eight. And I was the electrician. And I fixed all streets, the electric and other things. And that's what I made a living. Then I bought a truck. I bought a truck. And I was [INAUDIBLE] with that truck. The day when the war broke out, there was talking about a war. And they had called me to go to the service, because I was-- in 1924, I was in the service.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1920-- tell about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in [NON-ENGLISH]. My regiment was staying in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was in the service for two years. When I came back from the service, I started work electrician.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let me ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: I fixed here and there. And that was I made a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met my wife, and I had married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back to when you were in the Polish army. Were you in the Polish army with-- Russian army or Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish. And were there Jews and non-Jews in the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews? All of them, like here, Jewish and non-Jews is the same. Well, Saturdays, they used to take us to the shul, the Jews. And Sunday we have to wash the floors. And they take to the churches in the service. I was for two years in the service.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Jews treated differently than the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have no problems, was no problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you were telling me you got married in 1927. Were any children born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was born then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And five years later, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: So David was born in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know what was going on in Germany? What did you know about Hitler's rise to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before was Germany all right. Then they start-- when Hitler comes in 1933, they start chasing the Jews out. There was a lot of Jews coming out from Germany to Poland. And we helped them as much we could in 1933. So I had a truck, bought a truck. And I was transferring whoever needed, like a taxi. The day when the war broke out, I was in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I put up a whole barrel of gasoline. I knew it, gasoline we're not going to get. So I put a whole barrel of gasoline, put it on my truck. And I went back to Warsaw. And my truck was-- I covered up. Then they find truck, the police, Polish. And they put in wheat to-- to-- to take it to a mill to make-- not mill-- like we call a mill here.

speaker: interviewer
text: A factory? Flour?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flour, to make flour. And they took my truck and filled up with wheat. And they had to transfer 300 miles to the mill. And I was at home. I didn't know they took the truck. Then I go out. I see my truck is gone. I was running around to find it. The truck is loaded with wheat. And they take it to the mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took myself the truck. And on the road, I opened up the side and put all the wheat out on the ground until back to the-- there was a big risk-- and took back home and cover up the truck. That's how. So we wait for it's going to be further. Wednesday, it was Wednesday. The war started Friday. Tell me about that. What was that like? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: War, the German with the Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about where you were, and what happened, and how you knew what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was what it was. I covered up the truck. So when I took the truck back home, it was on Wednesday morning. I figured I had some relatives, not in Warsaw, but in our village about 500 miles away. So I figured Warsaw was not going to be good with no water and no food. We go in the village to my relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took my family, my brother-- my brother was on a bike going after me-- And took my family and took what I could on the truck. And I'm going on the road. On the road, my truck was run bad. But I had a barrel with gasoline. And this was a fortune. Was a [NON-ENGLISH] guy was in a new truck. And he didn't had no gasoline. So he says to me, give me the gasoline, and I take your family. On my truck was 17 people, strangers, just neighbors. I couldn't look. I says, I got 17 people. Can you take them? He says, no. Four people can I take.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I'm not going to leave anyone. Everybody's going with me. Where I am going to be, they going to be. I sold him the gasoline for cash. I sold him the gasoline for cash. And I went and bought a horse and wagon. So I had a horse and a wagon. But the wagon was too heavy. So at the next village, I traded in a light wagon. I paid off a guy. He give me a light one. And I loaded everything onto the wagon. And we go. In the daytime, we couldn't go because they bombed. They shot from the guns. They let the place go low and they shot. On the road was a lot of people killed. So we stay in the forests in the daytime. In the nighttime, we moved. In the daytime, I went and there was a time when potatoes was already in the land. I went and brought potatoes. I bought a big pot, a big one with two handles. And we cooked them in the forest. And we feed the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all 17 still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stranger people, but they was with me. I will not leave nobody, my neighbors from where I lived, and other I knew it. I knew the people. And that way, I went until Brest-Litovsk. And Brest-Litovsk was already Russian coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get across the border? Did you have any trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was no border this time. It was back and forth, the Germans, and then Poland. I met the Germans on the road. They give me cookies. They didn't bother me. They asked me if I got some guns. I says, I got a knife. They didn't bother. They give me-- they give cookies for the kids. And they went further. But when they go, they had a white flag that means they don't bother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back to before you left. You said the war started on Friday. And was your family all together when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we was together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there bombing? Did they come in? Was there bombing? And how did you know that the war had started, that they were attacking you? What were the Germans doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the war started, I wasn't already in Warsaw. I was already on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were on the road. Well, let me go back. You heard the people coming from Germany. And you knew that there were problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when you left the house, when you left your house, where did you think you were going? What were your plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking I go through a town [NON-ENGLISH]. And I had relatives there. And I was thinking, I'm going to stay in [NON-ENGLISH] with the relatives until the war wouldn't be too long, because Poland is not so big. But this wasn't like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when you left, what did you take? You had 17 people in your truck. What else did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people lately, everybody went for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did your family-- well, tell me who was in your family that left. You.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my brother. My brother was riding a bike after me. My brother's wife with a child, my wife with two kids, my wife's sisters, two girls, and some neighbors. I don't remember exactly where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take clothing with you? Did you take food? Did you take money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't took anything, because I was thinking I'll just go for a day or two. Even I didn't pick my father's picture from the wall. I was sorry leaving, but too late. I didn't took nothing. I just took our to cover up, pillow and a big-- how you call a big [INAUDIBLE] bed?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big blanket. That's what I took to cover up the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any money? Did you take money, silverware?

speaker: interviewee
text: Money was not much. Well, I sold the gasoline. I had money. I didn't have not much money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you were saying by the time you got to with the horse and buggy, where were you? With the horse and wagon, I was to [NON-ENGLISH]. From there, they were going to take away from me the horse. [LAUGHS] I had a fight with him, didn't give them. Who tried to take it away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Police. It wasn't the police. Everybody boys took a gun on the shoulder and he is police. And he wants to take away the horse. I says, no way. I was fighting with him. And I didn't give him the horse. Warsaw was far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the war stopped. No war. And I lived in Lutsk. I start working on a truck by an engineer Russian. There was already Russians. But the Russians took-- till Brest-Litovsk. Was Russian. And further was Germany. And I worked for that engineer. He wants to make a road from Kiev to Chelm to the Bug. So I was going with him making a road until May. May, I supposed to go to Lviv with him. But came an order. They're going to take all-- it was a lot of people from Warsaw, from the other side. Not from just from Warsaw, but from Poland. And that's on Lutsk. They're going to take all the people away from Lutsk. So the officer what I worked for says, we're not going to Lviv. We're going to pick all the people, strangers, from here. And they're going to take them somewhere, I don't know. But we pick up all these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to stop this tape now. We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
